# Soup Sieve

## Overview

Soup Sieve is a CSS selector library designed to be used with [Beautiful Soup 4][bs4]. It aims to provide selecting,
matching, and filtering using modern CSS selectors. Soup Sieve currently provides selectors from the CSS level 1
specifications up through the latest CSS level 4 drafts and beyond (though some are not yet implemented).

Soup Sieve was written with the intent to replace Beautiful Soup's builtin select feature, and as of Beautiful Soup
version 4.7.0, it now is :confetti_ball:. Soup Sieve can also be imported in order to use its API directly for
more controlled, specialized parsing.

Soup Sieve has implemented most of the CSS selectors up through the latest CSS draft specifications, though there are a
number that don't make sense in a non-browser environment. Selectors that cannot provide meaningful functionality simply
do not match anything. Some of the supported selectors are:

- `.classes`
- `#ids`
- `[attributes=value]`
- `parent child`
- `parent > child`
- `sibling ~ sibling`
- `sibling + sibling`
- `:not(element.class, element2.class)`
- `:is(element.class, element2.class)`
- `parent:has(> child)`
- and [many more](https://facelessuser.github.io/soupsieve/selectors/)


## Installation

You must have Beautiful Soup already installed:

```
pip install beautifulsoup4
```

In most cases, assuming you've installed version 4.7.0, that should be all you need to do, but if you've installed via
some alternative method, and Soup Sieve is not automatically installed, you can install it directly:

```
pip install soupsieve
```

If you want to manually install it from source, first ensure that [`build`](https://pypi.org/project/build/) is
installed:

```
pip install build
```

Then navigate to the root of the project and build the wheel and install (replacing `<ver>` with the current version):

```
python -m build -w
pip install dist/soupsive-<ver>-py3-none-any.whl
```

## Documentation

Documentation is found here: https://facelessuser.github.io/soupsieve/.

## License

functions.name: soupsieve.util.deprecated
documentation:
Raise a `DeprecationWarning` when wrapped function/method is called.

Usage:

    @deprecated("This method will be removed in version X; use Y instead.")
    def some_method()"
        pass

functions.name: soupsieve.__meta__.parse_version
documentation: Parse version into a comparable Version tuple.

classes.name: soupsieve.__meta__.Version
documentation:
Get the version (PEP 440).

A biased approach to the PEP 440 semantic version.

Provides a tuple structure which is sorted for comparisons `v1 > v2` etc.
  (major, minor, micro, release type, pre-release build, post-release build, development release build)
Release types are named in is such a way they are comparable with ease.
Accessors to check if a development, pre-release, or post-release build. Also provides accessor to get
development status for setup files.

How it works (currently):

- You must specify a release type as either `final`, `alpha`, `beta`, or `candidate`.
- To define a development release, you can use either `.dev`, `.dev-alpha`, `.dev-beta`, or `.dev-candidate`.
  The dot is used to ensure all development specifiers are sorted before `alpha`.
  You can specify a `dev` number for development builds, but do not have to as implicit development releases
  are allowed.
- You must specify a `pre` value greater than zero if using a prerelease as this project (not PEP 440) does not
  allow implicit prereleases.
- You can optionally set `post` to a value greater than zero to make the build a post release. While post releases
  are technically allowed in prereleases, it is strongly discouraged, so we are rejecting them. It should be
  noted that we do not allow `post0` even though PEP 440 does not restrict this. This project specifically
  does not allow implicit post releases.
- It should be noted that we do not support epochs `1!` or local versions `+some-custom.version-1`.

Acceptable version releases:

```
Version(1, 0, 0, "final")                    1.0
Version(1, 2, 0, "final")                    1.2
Version(1, 2, 3, "final")                    1.2.3
Version(1, 2, 0, ".dev-alpha", pre=4)        1.2a4
Version(1, 2, 0, ".dev-beta", pre=4)         1.2b4
Version(1, 2, 0, ".dev-candidate", pre=4)    1.2rc4
Version(1, 2, 0, "final", post=1)            1.2.post1
Version(1, 2, 3, ".dev")                     1.2.3.dev0
Version(1, 2, 3, ".dev", dev=1)              1.2.3.dev1
```

modules.name: soupsieve.pretty
documentation:
Format a pretty string of a `SoupSieve` object for easy debugging.

This won't necessarily support all types and such, and definitely
not support custom outputs.

It is mainly geared towards our types as the `SelectorList`
object is a beast to look at without some indentation and newlines.
The format and various output types is fairly known (though it
hasn't been tested extensively to make sure we aren't missing corners).

# Example:

\`
\>>> import soupsieve as sv
\>>> sv.compile('this > that.class\[name=value\]').selectors.pretty()
SelectorList(

> selectors=(
>
> : Selector(
>
>   : tag=SelectorTag(
>
>     : name='that',
>       prefix=None),
>
>     ids=(),
>     classes=(
>
>     > 'class',
>     > ),
>
>     attributes=(
>
>     : SelectorAttribute(
>
>       : attribute='name',
>         prefix='',
>         pattern=re.compile(
>
>         > '^value\$'),
>
>         xml_type_pattern=None),
>
>       ),
>
>     nth=(),
>     selectors=(),
>     relation=SelectorList(
>
>     > selectors=(
>     >
>     > : Selector(
>     >
>     >   : tag=SelectorTag(
>     >
>     >     : name='this',
>     >       prefix=None),
>     >
>     >     ids=(),
>     >     classes=(),
>     >     attributes=(),
>     >     nth=(),
>     >     selectors=(),
>     >     relation=SelectorList(
>     >
>     >     > selectors=(),
>     >     > is_not=False,
>     >     > is_html=False),
>     >
>     >     rel_type='>',
>     >     contains=(),
>     >     lang=(),
>     >     flags=0),
>     >
>     >   ),
>     >
>     > is_not=False,
>     > is_html=False),
>
>     rel_type=None,
>     contains=(),
>     lang=(),
>     flags=0),
>
>   ),
>
> is_not=False,
> is_html=False)

functions.name: soupsieve.css_parser.css_unescape
documentation:
Unescape CSS value.

Strings allow for spanning the value on multiple strings by escaping a new line.

classes.name: soupsieve.css_parser._Selector
documentation:
Intermediate selector class.

This stores selector data for a compound selector as we are acquiring them.
Once we are done collecting the data for a compound selector, we freeze
the data in an object that can be pickled and hashed.

methods.name: soupsieve.css_parser.CSSParser.parse_attribute_selector
documentation: Create attribute selector from the returned regex match.

methods.name: soupsieve.css_parser.CSSParser.parse_tag_pattern
documentation: Parse tag pattern from regex match.

methods.name: soupsieve.css_parser.CSSParser.parse_pseudo_class_custom
documentation:
Parse custom pseudo class alias.

Compile custom selectors as we need them. When compiling a custom selector,
set it to `None` in the dictionary so we can avoid an infinite loop.

classes.name: soupsieve.css_match._FakeParent
documentation:
Fake parent class.

When we have a fragment with no `BeautifulSoup` document object,
we can't evaluate `nth` selectors properly.  Create a temporary
fake parent so we can traverse the root element as a child.

methods.name: soupsieve.css_match._DocumentNav.assert_valid_input
documentation: Check if valid input tag or document.

methods.name: soupsieve.css_match._DocumentNav.is_content_string
documentation: Check if node is content string.

methods.name: soupsieve.css_match._DocumentNav.create_fake_parent
documentation: Create fake parent for a given element.

methods.name: soupsieve.css_match._DocumentNav.is_xml_tree
documentation: Check if element (or document) is from a XML tree.

methods.name: soupsieve.css_match._DocumentNav.is_root
documentation:
Return whether element is a root element.

We check that the element is the root of the tree (which we have already pre-calculated),
and we check if it is the root element under an `iframe`.

methods.name: soupsieve.css_match._DocumentNav.get_contents
documentation: Get contents or contents in reverse.

methods.name: soupsieve.css_match._DocumentNav.has_html_ns
documentation:
Check if element has an HTML namespace.

This is a bit different than whether a element is treated as having an HTML namespace,
like we do in the case of `is_html_tag`.

methods.name: soupsieve.css_match._DocumentNav.split_namespace
documentation: Return namespace and attribute name without the prefix.

methods.name: soupsieve.css_match._DocumentNav.normalize_value
documentation: Normalize the value to be a string or list of strings.

documentation: Class for parsing and validating input items.

methods.name: soupsieve.css_match.CSSMatch.match_attribute_name
documentation: Match attribute name and return value if it exists.

methods.name: soupsieve.css_match.CSSMatch.match_namespace
documentation: Match the namespace of the element.

methods.name: soupsieve.css_match.CSSMatch.match_contains
documentation: Match element if it contains text.

methods.name: soupsieve.css_match.CSSMatch.match_range
documentation:
Match range.

Behavior is modeled after what we see in browsers. Browsers seem to evaluate
if the value is out of range, and if not, it is in range. So a missing value
will not evaluate out of range; therefore, value is in range. Personally, I
feel like this should evaluate as neither in or out of range.

methods.name: soupsieve.css_match.CSSMatch.match_defined
documentation:
Match defined.

`:defined` is related to custom elements in a browser.

- If the document is XML (not XHTML), all tags will match.
- Tags that are not custom (don't have a hyphen) are marked defined.
- If the tag has a prefix (without or without a namespace), it will not match.

This is of course requires the parser to provide us with the proper prefix and namespace info,
if it doesn't, there is nothing we can do.

methods.name: soupsieve.css_match.CSSMatch.match_placeholder_shown
documentation:
Match placeholder shown according to HTML spec.

- text area should be checked if they have content. A single newline does not count as content.

methods.name: soupsieve.css_match.CSSMatch.match_selectors
documentation: Check if element matches one of the selectors.

methods.name: soupsieve.css_match.CSSMatch.select
documentation: Match all tags under the targeted tag.

classes.name: soupsieve.css_match.SoupSieve

methods.name: soupsieve.css_match.SoupSieve.filter
documentation:
Filter.

`CSSMatch` can cache certain searches for tags of the same document,
so if we are given a tag, all tags are from the same document,
and we can take advantage of the optimization.

Any other kind of iterable could have tags from different documents or detached tags,
so for those, we use a new `CSSMatch` for each item in the iterable.
Compiled Soup Sieve selector matching object.
